file_name: Jaworek-Korjakowska - 2015 - Novel method for border irregularity assessment in dermoscopic color images.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper presents a novel automatic algorithm for border irregularity assessment in dermoscopic color images, which is a key parameter in the ABCD rule for melanoma diagnosis. The method involves computational steps including image enhancement, lesion segmentation, borderline calculation, and irregularities detection using techniques such as lesion rotation, borderline division, and Gaussian low-pass filtering. The system achieved 92% accuracy on 350 dermoscopic images, demonstrating the use of computer vision and computational modeling to obtain diagnostic results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' in the header. The abstract and introduction describe the development and testing of a 'novel method' and 'new approach' for border irregularity assessment, involving original experimental work including algorithm design, implementation, and validation on a dataset of 350 dermoscopic images, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Novel Method for Border Irregularity Assessment in Dermoscopic Color Images
journal: Computational and Mathematical Methods in Medicine
brief_summary: This study presents a novel automatic algorithm for assessing border irregularity, a key parameter in the ABCD rule for melanoma diagnosis, using dermoscopic color images. The proposed method involves image enhancement, lesion segmentation, borderline calculation via lesion rotation and division, and irregularity detection using Gaussian low-pass filtering. The algorithm was evaluated on a dataset of 350 dermoscopic images containing 280 benign and 70 malignant cases. The system achieved an accuracy of 92%, with a sensitivity of 91% and precision of 89%, outperforming state-of-the-art methods. The results indicate that the computational approach effectively captures border irregularities and provides reliable information for skin mole examination. The authors conclude that this computer-aided system is a practical tool for both research and clinical applications in dermoscopic image assessment.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant melanoma is the primary focus of the study as the target condition for border irregularity assessment. The dataset contained 70 malignant cases (melanomas) out of 350 total images.

Benign melanocytic lesions (nevi) served as the comparator class. The dataset contained 280 benign cases out of 350 total images. The paper references a study by Nachbar et al. involving 103 melanocytic nevi.

all_task_types.details: The paper proposes an automatic algorithm for border irregularity assessment to aid in the diagnosis of pigmented skin lesions. The system classifies lesions as benign or malignant based on the ABCD rule criteria, specifically focusing on the border irregularity parameter. The study reports classification performance metrics including accuracy (92%), sensitivity (91%), and precision (89%) on a dataset of 350 dermoscopic images (280 benign, 70 malignant). While the algorithm calculates a continuous 'borderline function' and counts irregularities, these are features used for the final binary classification task.

datasets_used.name: Combined Dermoscopic Image Dataset
datasets_used.description: A collection of 350 dermoscopic images used to test the proposed border irregularity assessment algorithm. The images were sourced from two university hospitals (University of Naples, Italy, and University of Graz, Austria) stored on a CD-ROM, and a private database. Images were digitized using a Dermaphor apparatus and a Nikon F3 camera mounted on a stereomicroscope. All cases were based on histopathological examination of biopsy material. The dataset was curated by a dermatology expert with 10 years of experience, selecting images that fitted entirely in the frame and did not contain too many hairs.

use.testing: The algorithm was tested on the entire dataset of 350 images to evaluate border irregularity detection performance (sensitivity and precision).
use.other: The dataset was also used to assess the algorithm's ability to capture irregularities for effective skin mole examination, with results compared to state-of-the-art methods.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the proportion of border irregularities that were correctly detected by the algorithm compared to expert assessment.
To evaluate the proportion of detected irregularities that were true positives compared to all positive results.
To evaluate the overall correctness of the border irregularity detection algorithm.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy (92%), sensitivity (91%), and precision (89%).

Combined Dermoscopic Image Dataset (350.metric: Sensitivity and Precision by number of irregularities
Combined Dermoscopic Image Dataset (350.notes: The dataset consisted of 350 dermoscopic images (280 benign, 70 malignant). The algorithm achieved an overall accuracy of 92%. The paper notes that accuracy calculation was impossible for the irregularity detection task specifically because defining true negatives was hard, but reports 92% accuracy for the overall border irregularity assessment system. The proposed method obtained better results for border irregularities appearing not radially compared to approaches described in the literature.

pipeline_steps: Hair detection and inpainting (using NTSC 1953 grayscale conversion and black top-hat transform)
Skin lesion rotation (aligning major principal axis to horizontal)
Borderline function calculation (bounding box, center of mass, distance to image edge)
Irregularities detection (analyzing first derivative of borderline function)

classification_or_output_head.classification_method: Thresholding based on Total Dermoscopy Score (TDS) derived from ABCD rule parameters

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Proposed automatic algorithm (rule-based computer vision method)
preprocessing_techniques: Black frame removal (based on HSL lightness threshold < 15 and scanning rows for black pixels)
Hair detection using black top-hat transform
Hair distinction from other structures (dots, globules, pigment network) based on area, axes, and perimeter
Hair inpainting (replacing hair pixels with neighborhood average values)
Smoothing of healthy skin area during preprocessing
Skin lesion segmentation using seeded region-growing algorithm on healthy skin area
Skin lesion rotation to align major principal axis horizontally
conclusions: The proposed computer-aided system is a practical tool for dermoscopic image assessment and is recommended for both research and clinical applications.
The algorithm achieved an accuracy of 92% on 350 dermoscopic images, indicating it effectively captured most irregularities and provides reliable information for skin mole examination.
The proposed method obtained improved classification results compared to the state of the art, specifically performing better for border irregularities that do not appear radially.
The proposed algorithm can be applied in different fields of medical image analysis, including CT and MRI images.

The system can serve as part of a sophisticated screening tool for young inexperienced dermatologists and family physicians, aiding in the early detection of melanoma, particularly for people in remote areas.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation; the dataset was sourced from only two university hospitals (Naples, Italy; Graz, Austria) and a private database, limiting generalizability.
The dataset is significantly imbalanced, containing 280 benign cases versus only 70 malignant cases (20% prevalence), which may inflate accuracy metrics.

The algorithm was evaluated on a curated subset of 350 images selected by an expert to fit entirely in the frame and contain minimal hair, introducing selection bias and limiting applicability to real-world clinical images with challenging artifacts.

The paper explicitly states that accuracy is impossible to calculate for the specific border irregularity detection task because defining 'true negatives' is hard, yet reports 92% accuracy for the overall system, creating potential metric confusion.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; results are reported as descriptive metrics (sensitivity/precision) without significance testing.
Ground truth for border irregularity was determined manually by a single dermatology expert with 10 years of experience, introducing potential inter-observer variability and single-reader bias.

The method is rule-based and not a machine learning model, meaning it does not learn from data but relies on fixed geometric transformations (rotation, bounding box, Gaussian filtering), which may fail on lesions with atypical geometries not handled by the predefined rules.

The study compares the proposed method primarily against the radial distance method and other literature references, but does not provide a direct quantitative comparison against modern deep learning baselines or other contemporary state-of-the-art algorithms.
